sections.paragraph:
fut. יִנְהַג-

(1) pr. to pant, especially used of those who are exhausted by running; like the Syr. ܢܗܓ, Arab. نهج (kindred roots are הָגָה, נָאַק, נָהַק, compare Eth. ነሀከ፡ to be anxious, solicitous). See Piel, No. 1.

(2) causat, to urge on in a course, to drive (beasts). 2Ki 9:20 בִּי בְשִׁגָּעֹון יִנְהָֽג “for he drives (the horses) as if he were mad,” comes on at a most rapid rate, 2Ki 4:24 hence נָהַג עֲגָלָה to drive a wagon. 2Sa 6:3 followed by בְּ 1Ch 13:7 to lead or drive a flock (as a shepherd), Gen 31:18 Exo 3:1 followed by בְּ Isa 11:6 to drive away (cattle), Job 24:3 to lead any one, Son 8:2 to lead away captives, 1Sa 30:2 Isa 20:4 Comp. 60:11 . (With this signification accord Gr. Lat. ἄγω, ago, ἡγέομαι; Pers. اختن to lead, to bring).

(3) intransit. to act (etwas thun, treiben, handeln). Ecc 2:3, זְלִבִּי נֹהֵג בַּֽחָכְמָה “and my heart acting with wisdom.” I formerly explained this from the Ch. usage: “and my heart was accustomed to wisdom,” clave to it; but that now given is more simple.

Piel נִהַג, fut. יְנַהֵג-

(1) to pant, to sigh; see Kal No. 1, Nah 2:8.

(2) causat. of Kal No. 2. Exo 14:25, וַינֲהֲגֵהוּ בִּכְבֵדוּת “and caused to drive heavily.”

(3) i.q. Kal No. 2; to lead, Deu 4:27 28:37 to bring to, Exo 10:13 Psa 78:26 to lead away, Gen 31:26.

Derivative מִנְהָג.
